title: Bosses warn on ‘heavy-handed’ union AI role
full_text:
Labor’s capitulation on artificial intelligence to unions in its official platform would set back productivity and leave Australia behind the rest of the world, the country’s largest employer groups have warned Anthony Albanese and Jim Chalmers.
The Australian revealed on Sunday that the Labor Party’s draft policy platform – prepared ahead of the party’s national conference this month – commits the Albanese government to weigh up a new AI expert panel in the Fair Work Commission, a review of anti-discrimination law for AI, and ensuring human oversight for AI decisions in the public service.
It also endorses “co-design” between businesses and workers on the implementation of AI, which businesses argue would give unions an effective veto on AI rollout.
Businesses see the proliferation of AI as an opportunity to grow profits and productivity.
The Business Council of Australia warned overreach on the issue “risks putting Australia behind the rest of the world”.
“The Business Council’s Adopting AI in the Workplace guide sets out what responsible AI adoption looks like: genuine consultation, human accountability, clarity on the problem the technology is intended to solve, a practical approach to incorporating employee feedback, and a focus on skills and capability building to support better outcomes for both workers and businesses,” said chief executive Bran Black.
“What won’t help workers or businesses is a regime so complex and prescriptive that it stifles the very investment and innovation that creates the jobs and higher living standards Australians need.” Australian Chamber of Commerce and Industry chief executive Andrew McKellar said “co-design” was an “ominous term”.
“It would mean business owners having to share the steering wheel with union bosses when it comes to IT decisions,” he said.
“If that ends up being government policy, that would be a dead weight on productivity and put Australian businesses at a major disadvantage.” On Sunday, The Australian revealed unions had succeeded in pushing the party to take up more of their position on AI. Last year, the government released a “light touch” framework following business concerns that the Albanese government would use industrial relations to regulate AI.
Australian Industry Group chief executive Innes Willox said that a “lurch towards a union-directed, heavy-handed regulatory approach” would undermine employers’ rollout of AI in their workplaces and be “another major setback to productivity and our reputation as a home for long-term investment”. “We are still seeing the creep of union influence continue even after an avalanche of pro-union workplace changes over the past four years, most recently with new laws demanding companies hold union-agreed enterprise bargaining agreements to qualify for government procurement opportunities,” he said.
“The union push at the upcoming ALP national conference to control the implementation of artificial intelligence would simply turn employer attention to investing time, resources and personnel elsewhere where they are made to feel valued and welcome. We need to harness articulation intelligence (to) make ourselves more competitive and our workforce more skilled rather than turning our backs on it. The lessons of each technological advance since the Industrial Revolution in terms of job creation, better jobs, improved living standards and economic growth should be obvious to those who want to pretend Australia can ignore what is happening during this wave of change and not be rendered non-competitive and obsolete.” The Tech Council of Australia, which represents large tech companies, said it “supports an approach to the regulation and governance of AI in Australia that enables AI to be developed and adopted safely here, for the benefit of Australians”. “The tech sector is already doing significant practical work on AI safety, training, governance and responsible use, and policy should build on that,” a spokesperson said.
EDITORIAL P10 JUDITH SLOAN P11
